**Bowdoin College** ([/ˈboʊdɪn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-US-Bowdoin.ogg "File:En-US-Bowdoin.ogg") [*BOH-din*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [private](/wiki/Private_college "Private college") [liberal arts college](/wiki/Liberal_arts_colleges_in_the_United_States "Liberal arts colleges in the United States") in [Brunswick, Maine](/wiki/Brunswick,_Maine "Brunswick, Maine"), United States. It was chartered in 1794.

In addition to its Brunswick campus, Bowdoin owns a 118-acre (48 ha) coastal studies center on [Orr's Island](/wiki/Orr%27s_Island "Orr's Island")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and a 200-acre (81 ha) scientific field station on [Kent Island](/wiki/Kent_Island_(New_Brunswick) "Kent Island (New Brunswick)") in the [Bay of Fundy](/wiki/Bay_of_Fundy "Bay of Fundy").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The college was a founding member of its [athletic conference](/wiki/Athletic_conference "Athletic conference"), the [New England Small College Athletic Conference](/wiki/New_England_Small_College_Athletic_Conference "New England Small College Athletic Conference"), and the [Colby-Bates-Bowdoin Consortium](/wiki/Colby-Bates-Bowdoin_Consortium "Colby-Bates-Bowdoin Consortium"), an athletic conference and inter-library exchange with [Bates College](/wiki/Bates_College "Bates College") and [Colby College](/wiki/Colby_College "Colby College"). Bowdoin has over 30 varsity teams, and the school mascot was selected as a [polar bear](/wiki/Polar_bear "Polar bear") in 1913 to honor [Robert Peary](/wiki/Robert_Peary "Robert Peary"), a Bowdoin [alumnus](/wiki/List_of_Bowdoin_College_people "List of Bowdoin College people") who claimed to have led the first successful expedition to the North Pole.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Founding and 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Bowdoin_College_1845.jpeg/250px-Bowdoin_College_1845.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Bowdoin_College_1845.jpeg)

Bowdoin College, c. 1845 (Lithograph by [Fitz Hugh Lane](/wiki/Fitz_Henry_Lane "Fitz Henry Lane"))

Bowdoin College was chartered in 1794 by the [Massachusetts State Legislature](/wiki/Massachusetts_General_Court "Massachusetts General Court") and was later redirected under the jurisdiction of the [Maine Legislature](/wiki/Maine_Legislature "Maine Legislature").[[11]](#cite_note-11) It was named for former Massachusetts governor [James Bowdoin](/wiki/James_Bowdoin "James Bowdoin"), whose son [James Bowdoin III](/wiki/James_Bowdoin_III "James Bowdoin III") was an early benefactor.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Bowdoin began to develop in the 1820s, a decade in which Maine became an independent state as a result of the [Missouri Compromise](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise "Missouri Compromise"). The college graduated future U.S. President [Franklin Pierce](/wiki/Franklin_Pierce "Franklin Pierce") and two literary figures, [Nathaniel Hawthorne](/wiki/Nathaniel_Hawthorne "Nathaniel Hawthorne") and [Henry Wadsworth Longfellow](/wiki/Henry_Wadsworth_Longfellow "Henry Wadsworth Longfellow"), both of whom graduated [Phi Beta Kappa](/wiki/Phi_Beta_Kappa "Phi Beta Kappa") in 1825. Pierce and Hawthorne began an official militia company called the 'Bowdoin Cadets'.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The Phi Beta Kappa Society was active at Bowdoin before the Civil War and featured anti-slavery speakers.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Bowdoin_Medical_school.jpg/250px-Bowdoin_Medical_school.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bowdoin_Medical_school.jpg)

Bowdoin included the Medical School of Maine from 1821 to 1921

From its founding, Bowdoin was known to educate the sons of the political elite and "catered very largely to the wealthy conservative from the state of Maine".[[15]](#cite_note-15) During the first half of the 19th century, Bowdoin required of its students a certificate of "good moral character" as well as knowledge of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"), geography, algebra, and the major works of [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"), [Xenophon](/wiki/Xenophon "Xenophon"), [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil") and [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Harriet Beecher Stowe](/wiki/Harriet_Beecher_Stowe "Harriet Beecher Stowe") started writing her influential anti-slavery novel, *[Uncle Tom's Cabin](/wiki/Uncle_Tom%27s_Cabin "Uncle Tom's Cabin")*, in Brunswick while her husband was teaching at the college. [Brigadier General](/wiki/Brigadier_general_(United_States) "Brigadier general (United States)") (and [Brevet](/wiki/Brevet_(military) "Brevet (military)") [Major General](/wiki/Major_general_(United_States) "Major general (United States)")) [Joshua Chamberlain](/wiki/Joshua_Chamberlain "Joshua Chamberlain"), a Bowdoin alumnus and professor, was present at the surrender of the [Army of Northern Virginia](/wiki/Army_of_Northern_Virginia "Army of Northern Virginia") at [Appomattox Court House](/wiki/Appomattox_Court_House_National_Historical_Park "Appomattox Court House National Historical Park") in 1865. Chamberlain, a [Medal of Honor](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor "Medal of Honor") recipient who later served as [governor of Maine](/wiki/Governor_of_Maine "Governor of Maine") and president of Bowdoin, fought at [Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg"), where he commanded the [20th Maine](/wiki/20th_Maine "20th Maine") in defense of [Little Round Top](/wiki/Little_Round_Top "Little Round Top"). Other notable Civil War-era alumni include Major General [Oliver Otis Howard](/wiki/Oliver_Otis_Howard "Oliver Otis Howard"), class of 1850, who led the [Freedmen's Bureau](/wiki/Freedmen%27s_Bureau "Freedmen's Bureau") and later founded [Howard University](/wiki/Howard_University "Howard University"); Massachusetts Governor [John Andrew](/wiki/John_A._Andrew "John A. Andrew"), class of 1837, who was responsible for forming the [54th Massachusetts](/wiki/54th_Massachusetts "54th Massachusetts"); and [William P. Fessenden](/wiki/William_P._Fessenden "William P. Fessenden") (1823) and [Hugh McCulloch](/wiki/Hugh_McCulloch "Hugh McCulloch") (1827), both of whom served as Secretary of the Treasury during the [Lincoln Administration](/wiki/Lincoln_Administration "Lincoln Administration").

With strained relations over slavery between political parties, President Franklin Pierce appointed [Jefferson Davis](/wiki/Jefferson_Davis "Jefferson Davis") as his [Secretary of War](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_War "United States Secretary of War"), and the college awarded the future [President of the Confederacy](/wiki/President_of_the_Confederate_States_of_America "President of the Confederate States of America") an honorary degree. The Jefferson Davis Award was established in 1972 following a donation from the [United Daughters of the Confederacy](/wiki/United_Daughters_of_the_Confederacy "United Daughters of the Confederacy") and was given to students excelling in legal studies.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The award was discontinued in 2015, with the college president citing it as inappropriate because it honored someone "whose mission was to preserve and institutionalize slavery".[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Although Bowdoin's Medical School of Maine closed in 1921,[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19) it produced notable graduates including [Augustus Stinchfield](/wiki/Augustus_Stinchfield "Augustus Stinchfield"), who received his [M.D.](/wiki/Doctor_of_Medicine "Doctor of Medicine") in 1868 and became one of the co-founders of the [Mayo Clinic](/wiki/Mayo_Clinic "Mayo Clinic") in [Rochester, Minnesota](/wiki/Rochester,_Minnesota "Rochester, Minnesota"). In 1877, the college graduated [Charles Morse](/wiki/Charles_W._Morse "Charles W. Morse"), the American banker who established a near-monopoly of the ice business in New York, which contributed to the [financial Panic of 1907](/wiki/Panic_of_1907 "Panic of 1907").[[20]](#cite_note-Google_Books-20)

The college educated and graduated Arctic explorers [Robert E. Peary](/wiki/Robert_E._Peary "Robert E. Peary"), class of 1877, and [Donald B. MacMillan](/wiki/Donald_B._MacMillan "Donald B. MacMillan"), class of 1898. Peary named [Bowdoin Fjord](/wiki/Bowdoin_Fjord "Bowdoin Fjord") and [Bowdoin Glacier](/wiki/Bowdoin_Glacier "Bowdoin Glacier") after his alma mater.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Peary led what he claimed was the first successful expedition to reach the North Pole in April 1909,[[22]](#cite_note-22) and MacMillan, a member of Peary's crew, explored [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), [Baffin Island](/wiki/Baffin_Island "Baffin Island"), and [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador") in the [schooner *Bowdoin*](/wiki/Bowdoin_(Arctic_schooner) "Bowdoin (Arctic schooner)") between 1908 and 1954.

[Wallace H. White, Jr.](/wiki/Wallace_H._White,_Jr. "Wallace H. White, Jr."), class of 1899, served as Senate Minority Leader from 1944 to 1947 and Senate Majority Leader from 1947 to 1949. [George J. Mitchell](/wiki/George_J._Mitchell "George J. Mitchell"), class of 1954, served as Senate Majority Leader from 1989 to 1995 before assuming an active role in the Northern Ireland peace process.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1970, the college became one of a very limited number of liberal arts colleges to make the [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") optional in the admissions process. In 1971, after nearly 180 years as a men's college, Bowdoin admitted its first class of women. Also in 1971, Bowdoin became a founding member of the [New England Small College Athletic Conference](/wiki/New_England_Small_College_Athletic_Conference "New England Small College Athletic Conference") (NESCAC) and began competing in the [Colby-Bates-Bowdoin Consortium](/wiki/Colby-Bates-Bowdoin_Consortium "Colby-Bates-Bowdoin Consortium") with [Bates College](/wiki/Bates_College "Bates College") and [Colby College](/wiki/Colby_College "Colby College"). The consortium became both an athletic rivalry and an academic exchange program. On February 28, 1997, Bowdoin's Board of Trustees approved a plan to phase out fraternities on campus,[[24]](#cite_note-24) replacing them with a system of college-owned social houses.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Campus_view%28tower%29.jpg/250px-Campus_view%28tower%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campus_view(tower).jpg)

View of the campus from Coles Tower

On January 18, 2008, Bowdoin announced that it would eliminate loans for all students receiving financial aid, replacing those loans with grants beginning with the 2008–2009 academic year.[[26]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-26) President [Barry Mills](/wiki/Barry_Mills_(college_president) "Barry Mills (college president)") stated, "Some see a calling in such vital but often low-paying fields such as teaching or social work. With significant debt at graduation, some students will undoubtedly be forced to make career or education choices not based on their talents, interests, and promise in a particular field but rather on their capacity to repay student loans. As an institution devoted to the common good, Bowdoin must consider the fairness of such a result."[[26]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-26)

In February 2009, following a $10 million donation by [Subway](/wiki/Subway_(restaurant) "Subway (restaurant)") co-founder and alumnus [Peter Buck](/wiki/Peter_Buck_(restaurateur) "Peter Buck (restaurateur)"), class of 1952, the college completed a $250 million capital campaign. The college also completed major construction projects on campus, including a renovation of the art museum and a new fitness center named after Peter Buck.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

On July 1, 2015, [Clayton Rose](/wiki/Clayton_Rose "Clayton Rose") succeeded Mills as president.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Eight years later, on July 1, 2023, [Safa Zaki](/wiki/Safa_Zaki "Safa Zaki") succeeded Rose as the first woman to serve as president of the college.

In February 2025, students occupied the Smith Union building to demand that the university divest from arms manufacturers, marking the first [pro-Palestinian encampment](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses") during the [second Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump").[[29]](#cite_note-29) The encampment followed Trump's comments on occupying Gaza and perceived inaction following an Israel divestment referendum passed by the student body a year prior.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Admissions")]

The acceptance rate for the class of 2029 was 6.8%. The college received more than 14,000 applications for the class of 2029 – the highest application number in the college's history and a continuation of the rise in applications over the past three years. Applications increased by about 50%, since the class of 2024 and about 100% since the class of 2021.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

*[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* classifies Bowdoin as "most selective".[[34]](#cite_note-34) Of enrolling students, 89% are in the top 10% of their high school graduating class.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Although Bowdoin does not require the [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") in admissions, all students may submit a score upon application. The middle 50% SAT range for the verbal and math sections of the [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") is 660–750 and 660–750, respectively—numbers of only those submitting scores during the admissions process. The middle 50% ACT range is 30–33.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The April 17, 2008, edition of *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* noted Bowdoin in an article on university admissions: "So-called 'almost-Ivies' such as Bowdoin and Middlebury also saw record low admission rates this year (18% each). It is now as hard to get into Bowdoin, says the college's admissions director, as it was to get into Princeton in the 1970s."[[37]](#cite_note-37) Many students apply for financial aid, and around 85% of those who apply to receive aid. Bowdoin is a [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") and no-loans institution.[[26]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-26) While a significant portion of the student body hails from [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England")—including nearly 25% from Massachusetts and 10% from Maine—recent classes have drawn from an increasingly national and international pool. The median family income of Bowdoin students is $195,900, with 57% of students coming from the top 10% of highest-earning families and 17.5% from the bottom 60%.[[38]](#cite_note-NYT_mobility_index-38) Although Bowdoin once had a reputation for homogeneity (both ethnically and socioeconomically), a diversity campaign has increased the percentage of students of color in recent classes to more than 31%.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In fact, admission of minorities goes back at least as far as [John Brown Russwurm](/wiki/John_Brown_Russwurm "John Brown Russwurm") 1826, Bowdoin's first Black college graduate and the third Black graduate of any American college.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Bowdoin-chapel-winter.jpg/250px-Bowdoin-chapel-winter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bowdoin-chapel-winter.jpg)

Bowdoin Chapel during the winter semester

Course distribution requirements were abolished in the 1970s but were reinstated by a faculty majority vote in 1981 due to an initiative by oral communication and film professor Barbara Kaster. She insisted that distribution requirements would ensure students a more well-rounded education in a diversity of fields and therefore present them with more career possibilities. The requirements of at least two courses in each of the categories of [Natural Sciences](/wiki/Natural_Sciences "Natural Sciences")/mathematics, [social and behavioral sciences](/wiki/Behavioral_Sciences "Behavioral Sciences"), [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities")/[Fine Arts](/wiki/Fine_Arts "Fine Arts"), and foreign studies (including languages) took effect for the class of 1987 and have been gradually amended since then. Current requirements require one course each in natural sciences, quantitative reasoning, visual and performing arts, international perspectives, and difference, power, and inequity. A small writing-intensive course, called a first-year seminar, is also required.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The most popular majors, by 2021 graduates, were:[[42]](#cite_note-42)

:   :   Political Science and Government (82)
    :   Econometrics and Quantitative Economics (61)
    :   Biology/Biological Sciences (30)
    :   Biochemistry (28)
    :   Neuroscience (25)
    :   English Language and Literature (25)
    :   Mathematics (25)

In 1990, the Bowdoin faculty voted to change the four-level grading system to the traditional A, B, C, D, and F system.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The previous system, consisting of high honors, honors, pass, and fail, was devised primarily to de-emphasize the importance of grades and to reduce competition.[[44]](#cite_note-44) In 2002, the faculty decided to change the grading system to incorporate plus and minus grades. In 2006, Bowdoin was named a "Top Producer of [Fulbright Awards](/wiki/Fulbright_Award "Fulbright Award") for American Students" by the Institute of International Education.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Other notable Bowdoin faculty include (or have included): [Edville Gerhardt Abbott](/wiki/Edville_Gerhardt_Abbott "Edville Gerhardt Abbott"), [Charles Beitz](/wiki/Charles_Beitz "Charles Beitz"), [John Bisbee](/wiki/John_Bisbee "John Bisbee"), [Paul Chadbourne](/wiki/Paul_Chadbourne "Paul Chadbourne"), [Thomas Cornell](/wiki/Thomas_Cornell_(artist) "Thomas Cornell (artist)"), [Kristen R. Ghodsee](/wiki/Kristen_R._Ghodsee "Kristen R. Ghodsee"), [Eddie Glaude](/wiki/Eddie_Glaude "Eddie Glaude"), [Joseph E. Johnson](/wiki/Joseph_E._Johnson_(government_official) "Joseph E. Johnson (government official)"), [Richard Morgan](/wiki/Richard_E._Morgan "Richard E. ## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Student life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Hubbard_Hall_-_Bowdoin_College_-_IMG_7782.JPG/250px-Hubbard_Hall_-_Bowdoin_College_-_IMG_7782.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hubbard_Hall_-_Bowdoin_College_-_IMG_7782.JPG)

Hubbard Hall, once the college's library

Bowdoin's dining services have been ranked No. 1 among all universities and colleges nationally by *[Princeton Review](/wiki/Princeton_Review "Princeton Review")* in 2004, 2006, 2007, 2011, 2013, 2014, and 2016,[[59]](#cite_note-59) with *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reporting: "If it weren't for the trays, and for the fact that most diners are under 25, you'd think it was a restaurant."[[60]](#cite_note-60)

Bowdoin uses food from its organic garden in its two major dining halls, and every academic year begins with a lobster bake outside Farley Fieldhouse.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Recalling his days at Bowdoin in a 2005 interview, Professor [Richard E. Morgan](/wiki/Richard_E._Morgan "Richard E. Morgan") (class of 1959) described student life at the then-all-male school as "monastic" and noted that "the only things to do were either work or drink". This is corroborated by the *[Official Preppy Handbook](/wiki/Official_Preppy_Handbook "Official Preppy Handbook"),* which in 1980 ranked Bowdoin the number two drinking school in the country, behind [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College"). These days, Morgan observed, the college offers a far broader array of recreational opportunities: "If we could have looked forward in time to Bowdoin's standard of living today, we would have been astounded."[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Since abolishing Greek [fraternities](/wiki/Fraternities "Fraternities") in the late 1990s, Bowdoin has switched to a system in which entering students are assigned a "college house" affiliation correlating with their first-year dormitory.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] While six houses were originally established following the construction of two new dorms, two were added effective in the fall of 2007, and one added in the fall of 2019, bringing the current total to eight: Baxter, Quinby, MacMillan, Howell, Helmreich, Reed, Burnett, and Boody-Johnson. The college houses are physical buildings around campus that host parties and other events throughout the year. Those students who choose not to live in their affiliated house retain their affiliation and are considered members throughout their Bowdoin career.

The largest student group on campus is the Outing Club, which leads canoeing, kayaking, rafting, camping, and backpacking trips throughout Maine.[[63]](#cite_note-63) One of the school's two historic rival literary societies, The Peucinian Society, has recently been revitalized from its previous form. The Peucinian Society was founded in 1805.[[64]](#cite_note-64) This organization counts such people as [Henry Wadsworth Longfellow](/wiki/Henry_Wadsworth_Longfellow "Henry Wadsworth Longfellow") and [Joshua Lawrence Chamberlain](/wiki/Joshua_Chamberlain "Joshua Chamberlain") among its former members. The other, the now-defunct Athenian Society, included [Nathaniel Hawthorne](/wiki/Nathaniel_Hawthorne "Nathaniel Hawthorne") and [Franklin Pierce](/wiki/Franklin_Pierce "Franklin Pierce") as members.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bowdoin competes in the Standard Platform League of [RoboCup](/wiki/RoboCup "RoboCup") as the Northern Bites, where teams compete with five autonomous [Aldebaran Nao](/wiki/Nao_(robot) "Nao (robot)") robots. Bowdoin won the world championship in RoboCup 2007, beating [Carnegie Mellon University](/wiki/Carnegie_Mellon_University "Carnegie Mellon University"), and finished 2nd in the 2015 US Open.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### Media and publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowdoin_College&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Media and publications")]

Bowdoin's student newspaper, *[The Bowdoin Orient](/wiki/The_Bowdoin_Orient "The Bowdoin Orient")*, is the oldest continuously published college weekly in the United States.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The Orient was named the second-best tabloid-sized college weekly at a Collegiate Associated Press conference in March 2007 and the best college newspaper in New England by the New England Society of News Editors in 2018.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The school's literary magazine, *[The Quill](/wiki/The_Quill_(Bowdoin) "The Quill (Bowdoin)")*, was published between 1897 and 2015. *The Bowdoin Globalist*, an international news, culture, and politics magazine affiliated with the Global21 organization of college magazines, has been publishing since 2012. *The Bowdoin Globalist* transitioned to a digital-only platform in 2015 and changed its name to *The Bowdoin Review*. The college's radio station, [WBOR](/wiki/WBOR "WBOR"), has been operating since the early 1940s. In 1999, *The Bowdoin Cable Network* was formed, producing a weekly newscast and several student-created shows per semester.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Bowdoin College signed onto the American College and University President's Climate Commitment in 2007.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The college followed through with a [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") plan released in 2009, with 2020 as the target year for carbon neutrality. According to the plan, general improvements to Maine's electricity grid will account for 7% of carbon reductions, commuting improvements will account for 1%, and the purchase of renewable energy credits will account for 41%.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The plan includes the construction of a solar thermal system, part of the "Thorne Solar Hot Water Project"; cogeneration in the central heating plant (for which Bowdoin received $400,000 in federal grants); lighting upgrades to all campus buildings; and modern monitoring systems of energy usage on campus.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In 2017, the college was on track to meet the 28% own source reduction target, and efforts have continued in the areas of energy conservation, efficiency upgrades and transitioning to lower carbon fuel sources.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Bowdoin's facilities are heated by an on-campus heating plant that burns natural gas.[[77]](#cite_note-bowdoin.edu-77)

Between 2002 and 2008, Bowdoin College decreased its CO2 emissions by 40%. It achieved that reduction by switching from No. 6 to No. 2 oil in its heating plant, reducing the campus set heating point from 72 to 68 degrees, and by adhering to its own Green Design Standards in renovations.[[78]](#cite_note-What_We're_Doing-78) In addition, Bowdoin runs a [single stream recycling](/wiki/Single_stream_recycling "Single stream recycling") program, and its dining services department has begun composting [food waste](/wiki/Food_waste "Food waste") and unbleached paper napkins.[[79]](#cite_note-Waste_Management-79) Bowdoin received an overall "B−" grade for its sustainability efforts on the College Sustainability Report Card 2009 published by the Sustainable Endowments Institute.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

In 2003, Bowdoin committed to achieving [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environment_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environment Design")-certification for all new campus buildings.[[81]](#cite_note-bowdoin1-81) The college has since completed construction on Osher and West residency halls, the Peter Buck Center for Health & Fitness, the [Sidney J. Watson Arena](/wiki/Sidney_J._Watson_Arena "Sidney J. Watson Arena"), 216 Maine Street, and 52 Harpswell all of which have attained LEED, Silver LEED or Gold LEED certification. The new dorms partially use collected rainwater as part of an advanced flushing system.[[81]](#cite_note-bowdoin1-81) The new Park Row Apartments and Schiller Coastal Studies Center are Passive House Certified through Phius.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Organized athletics at Bowdoin began in 1828[[87]](#cite_note-87) with a gymnastics program established by the "father of athletics in Maine",[[88]](#cite_note-88) [John Neal](/wiki/John_Neal "John Neal"). In the proceeding years, Neal agitated for more programs, and himself taught [bowling](/wiki/Bowling "Bowling"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), and other sports.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Bowdoin College teams are known as the Polar Bears. They compete as a member of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") level, primarily competing in the [New England Small College Athletic Conference](/wiki/New_England_Small_College_Athletic_Conference "New England Small College Athletic Conference") (NESCAC), of which they were a founding member in 1971.

The mascot for all Bowdoin College athletic teams is the [Polar bear](/wiki/Polar_bear "Polar bear"), generally referred to in the plural, i.e., "The Polar Bears". The uniform color is white.[[7]](#cite_note-Athletics_Quick_Facts-7) The fight song, *Forward The White*, was composed by Kenneth A. Robinson, class of 1914.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

The college's rowing club competes in the [Colby-Bates-Bowdoin Chase Regatta](/wiki/Colby-Bates-Bowdoin_Chase_Regatta "Colby-Bates-Bowdoin Chase Regatta") annually. The rowing club also competes as a member of the American Collegiate Rowing Association; the women's team won its first national championship in 2023.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The field hockey team are four-time NCAA Division III National Champions; winning the title in 2007 (defeating [Middlebury College](/wiki/Middlebury_College "Middlebury College")), 2008 (defeating [Tufts University](/wiki/Tufts_University "Tufts University")), 2010 (defeating [Messiah College](/wiki/Messiah_College "Messiah College")), and 2013 (defeating [Salisbury University](/wiki/Salisbury_University#Athletics "Salisbury University")).[[92]](#cite_note-92) The men's tennis team won the 2016 NCAA Division III Championship after defeating Emory University in [Kalamazoo, Michigan](/wiki/Kalamazoo,_Michigan "Kalamazoo, Michigan").[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Principal athletic facilities include [Whittier Field](/wiki/Whittier_Field "Whittier Field") (capacity: 9,000), Morrell Gymnasium (1,500), [Sidney J. Watson Arena](/wiki/Sidney_J._Watson_Arena "Sidney J. Watson Arena") (2,300), Pickard Fields, and the Buck Center for Health and Wellness.

Bowdoin graduates have led all three branches of the American federal government, including both houses of Congress. [Franklin Pierce](/wiki/Franklin_Pierce "Franklin Pierce") (1824) was America's fourteenth President; [Melville Weston Fuller](/wiki/Melville_Weston_Fuller "Melville Weston Fuller") (1853) served as [Chief Justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States"); [Thomas Brackett Reed](/wiki/Thomas_Brackett_Reed "Thomas Brackett Reed") (1860) was twice elected [Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"); and [Wallace H. White, Jr.](/wiki/Wallace_H._White,_Jr. "Wallace H. White, Jr.") (1899) and [George J. Mitchell](/wiki/George_J._Mitchell "George J. Mitchell") (1954) both served as [Majority Leader](/wiki/Senate_Majority_Leader "Senate Majority Leader") of the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
